summary: Evans explores the landscape of running containers in production, comparing Docker and rkt while candidly admitting she has many unanswered questions. She identifies packaging as the most compelling reason to use containers, since installing dependencies on servers is error-prone and painful. She raises concerns about Docker's daemon architecture, where bugs can deadlock and kill all containers, versus rkt's simpler process model. The post also surfaces subtle gotchas like PID 1 behavior, networking complexity, and secrets management. Her overall assessment from talking to practitioners is that containers are genuinely useful despite the hype, but full of sharp edges.
key_points: Packaging is the killer feature of containers — installing dependencies correctly on servers is hard and scary, and containers solve this cleanly
Docker's daemon architecture is a liability: if the daemon deadlocks, all containers die, whereas rkt runs containers as regular processes
Containers violate normal Unix assumptions, such as your process unexpectedly becoming PID 1 and inheriting zombie processes
Container networking adds complexity that may not always be necessary — sometimes you just want normal ports in a normal network namespace
The practitioner consensus is that containers are useful despite the hype, but have many sharp edges to navigate
money_quotes: Installing stuff on computers so you can run your program on them really sucks. It's easy to get wrong! It's scary when you make changes!
I don't want to have to trust that the Docker daemon has no bugs that will affect me. All software has bugs!
I think 'violates a lot of normal Unix assumptions about what normally happens to normal processes' is basically the whole story about containers.
So far reading about containers mostly feels like this hilarious article.
You don't want your process to get random zombie processes as children. Like that might be fine, but it violates a lot of normal Unix assumptions.
Do you really need to use this complicated container networking stuff? I kind of just want to run container processes and run them on normal ports in my normal network namespace and leave it at that.
key_insight: Containers solve the real pain of packaging and deployment, but their abstractions violate familiar Unix assumptions in ways that create surprising sharp edges in production.
